questions.stem: A 45-year-old previously healthy man presents to the emergency department with a sudden loss of hearing in his right ear. He noticed it upon waking this morning. He denies any vertigo, tinnitus, or ear pain. Otoscopy is normal. Rinne's test is positive bilaterally. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Idiopathic SSNHL is a diagnosis of exclusion in a patient with sudden unilateral hearing loss and normal otoscopy. The absence of vertigo makes Meniere's disease and viral labyrinthitis less likely. Acoustic neuroma typically presents with gradual hearing loss and tinnitus.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with a two-day history of severe pain in her left ear, followed by sudden hearing loss and the appearance of small blisters on the outer ear. On examination, she has vesicles in the left ear canal and on the pinna, with mild ipsilateral facial weakness. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of otalgia, vesicular rash in the ear, and facial nerve palsy is diagnostic of Ramsay Hunt syndrome. Hearing loss is due to involvement of the vestibulocochlear nerve. Other causes do not produce the characteristic rash.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the GP with a 12-hour history of vertigo, nausea, and a feeling of fullness in her left ear. She had a similar episode three months ago that lasted a few hours. She also reports a recent low-pitched tinnitus in the same ear. On examination, she has horizontal nystagmus. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Episodic vertigo lasting hours, with tinnitus, aural fullness, and fluctuating hearing loss, is characteristic of Meniere's disease. Viral labyrinthitis typically presents with a single prolonged episode. BPPV lacks hearing loss and tinnitus.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a known history of chronic suppurative otitis media with cholesteatoma in his left ear presents with acute onset of vertigo and a severe drop in hearing in that ear. On examination, he has horizontal nystagmus and a positive fistula sign. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In the context of cholesteatoma, sudden hearing loss with vertigo and a positive fistula sign (pressure-induced nystagmus) is highly suggestive of a labyrinthine fistula. The cholesteatoma erodes the bony labyrinth, creating a communication with the inner ear.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia presents with sudden unilateral hearing loss in his right ear. He also feels slightly unsteady but denies true vertigo. He had a transient ischaemic attack six months ago. Otoscopy is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Given the age and multiple cardiovascular risk factors, a vascular cause (labyrinthine infarction due to AICA ischaemia) is more likely than idiopathic SSNHL. The absence of vertigo and presence of vascular risk factors tilt the diagnosis away from Meniere's or labyrinthitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset hearing loss in her right ear, occurring over a few hours. She has no otalgia, no vertigo, and no history of recent trauma. Otoscopy reveals a normal tympanic membrane. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of sudden sensorineural hearing loss?
options: CT scan of the temporal bones
MRI of the internal auditory meatus
questions.explanation: Pure tone audiometry is the first-line investigation to confirm sensorineural hearing loss and quantify its severity. It distinguishes from conductive loss and documents the threshold required for diagnosis (≥30 dB across three contiguous frequencies). MRI is indicated if there is no improvement or atypical features, but not for initial confirmation.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man reports a sudden loss of hearing in his left ear while at work yesterday. Pure tone audiometry shows a hearing threshold of 25 dB at 500 Hz, 30 dB at 1000 Hz, and 35 dB at 2000 Hz. According to standard diagnostic criteria, does this patient meet the definition of sudden sensorineural hearing loss?
options: No, because the loss is less than 40 dB
Yes, because there is a loss of at least 30 dB over three consecutive frequencies
Yes, because there is a loss of at least 30 dB at one frequency
No, because the criteria require a loss of ≥30 dB at three consecutive frequencies, and here only two frequencies reach 30 dB or more
No, because the loss does not involve 3000 Hz
questions.explanation: The standard diagnostic threshold for sudden sensorineural hearing loss is sensorineural hearing loss of ≥30 dB over at least three contiguous frequencies occurring within 72 hours. In this audiogram, only two frequencies (1000 and 2000 Hz) have thresholds ≥30 dB; 500 Hz is 25 dB. Therefore, it does not meet criteria. Option A is incorrect because one frequency is insufficient; B is incorrect because only two frequencies meet threshold. C and D are not correct criteria.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presents with sudden profound hearing loss in her left ear. Pure tone audiometry reveals no response at any frequency in that ear (complete anacusis). The tympanic membrane appears normal. Which investigation is the most appropriate next step to investigate the cause?
options: CT scan of the head without contrast
MRI of the internal auditory meatus with gadolinium
questions.explanation: In cases of sudden profound hearing loss (especially anacusis), the priority is to exclude a retrocochlear lesion such as a vestibular schwannoma. MRI with gadolinium of the internal auditory meatus is the gold standard for this. Repeat audiometry is not urgent. CT head is less sensitive for intracanalicular tumours. Electrocochleography and VEMP are not first-line for cause investigation.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with sudden hearing loss in his right ear following a viral upper respiratory tract infection. Audiometry shows a flat sensorineural hearing loss of 60 dB across all frequencies. Which audiometric pattern is most consistent with a diagnosis of sudden sensorineural hearing loss rather than noise-induced hearing loss?
questions.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss typically presents with a notch at 4 kHz (option A). Sudden sensorineural hearing loss most commonly has a flat or down-sloping pattern, but a flat loss (equal across frequencies) is highly characteristic of SSNHL, especially when acute. Options B, C, and E are less typical; low-frequency loss is more associated with Meniere's disease, high-frequency down-sloping with presbycusis, and cookie-bite with genetic or congenital loss.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with sudden hearing loss in his left ear immediately after a rugby tackle. Otoscopy is normal, but there is a suspicion of a perilymphatic fistula. Which investigation is most appropriate to identify a temporal bone fracture as a potential cause?
options: High-resolution CT of the temporal bones
questions.explanation: In the setting of trauma, a temporal bone fracture may cause a perilymphatic fistula leading to sudden sensorineural hearing loss. High-resolution CT of the temporal bones is the best imaging to identify fractures, especially of the otic capsule. MRI may show labyrinthine hemorrhage but is less sensitive for fractures. Fistula test is clinical, not imaging. Audiometry and ENG are functional tests.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old otherwise healthy woman presents with sudden-onset unilateral hearing loss over 24 hours. Pure tone audiometry shows a 40 dB loss at low frequencies. She has no vertigo, otalgia, or ear discharge. Idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) is diagnosed. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
Oral prednisolone 60 mg daily for 7 days
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) as first-line therapy for idiopathic SSNHL within 2 weeks of onset. Intratympanic steroids are reserved for salvage or when systemic steroids are contraindicated. Intravenous steroids, hyperbaric oxygen, and antivirals are not first-line for idiopathic cases.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 95 mmol/mol) presents with profound sudden sensorineural hearing loss (no detectable hearing on audiometry) in the right ear of 2 days’ duration. He has no other neurological symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In patients with poorly controlled diabetes, systemic corticosteroids risk significant hyperglycaemia. Intratympanic steroids deliver high local concentrations with minimal systemic absorption, making them the preferred first-line option. Oral or IV steroids are relatively contraindicated. Hyperbaric oxygen is not first-line, and no treatment is not appropriate.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with sudden left-sided hearing loss and vertigo. On examination, there are vesicular lesions on the left pinna and external auditory canal. Pure tone audiometry confirms a sensorineural hearing loss. What is the single most important first-line treatment?
options: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
questions.explanation: The vesicular rash indicates Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster virus reactivation). Antiviral therapy (oral aciclovir or valaciclovir) should be started promptly to reduce the risk of postherpetic neuralgia and improve hearing outcomes. Steroids are adjunctive but not the most important single therapy; the antiviral targets the cause.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a history of untreated latent tuberculosis (positive IGRA, no active disease) presents with sudden sensorineural hearing loss of 3 days’ duration. Idiopathic SSNHL is diagnosed. Which treatment is relatively contraindicated?
options: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
questions.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids can reactivate latent TB; they are avoided unless TB is adequately treated or prophylaxis is given. Intratympanic steroids have minimal systemic effects and are safer. Hyperbaric oxygen and antivirals are not first-line and do not pose this risk.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss completed a 7-day course of oral prednisolone 60 mg daily with minimal improvement (persistent 50 dB loss). What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat a 7-day course of oral prednisolone
questions.explanation: NICE recommends intratympanic steroids as salvage therapy for patients with incomplete recovery after oral steroids. Repeating oral steroids is not supported, hyperbaric oxygen lacks robust evidence, antivirals are not indicated for idiopathic cases, and no treatment is inappropriate when salvage options exist.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of sudden hearing loss in her left ear. She has no vertigo, tinnitus, or ear pain. Otoscopy is normal. Pure tone audiometry confirms a sensorineural hearing loss of >30 dB in three contiguous frequencies in the left ear. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent MRI internal auditory meatus
Start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend high-dose oral corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone 60 mg daily) as first-line treatment for sudden sensorineural hearing loss, ideally started within 72 hours. MRI is indicated if there is no recovery after treatment or if atypical features are present, not as an immediate initial step. Observation is only acceptable if the hearing loss is very mild and the patient declines treatment.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (on metformin) presents with sudden hearing loss in his right ear and tinnitus for 2 days. Otoscopy is normal. Audiometry shows a 30 dB sensorineural hearing loss across low frequencies. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise observation and repeat audiometry in 1 week
Start oral prednisolone 60 mg daily with blood glucose monitoring
Check viral serology (e.g. EBV, CMV, HSV)
questions.explanation: Oral prednisolone remains first-line treatment even in diabetes, but blood glucose must be monitored closely. Intratympanic injection is reserved for salvage therapy after failed oral steroids or contraindications. Observation is not recommended as early treatment improves outcomes. Viral serology is not routinely indicated unless atypical features are present.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset of profound hearing loss in her left ear, severe vertigo, vomiting, and horizontal nystagmus. Otoscopy is normal. She has no focal neurological signs. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g. methylprednisolone) are indicated for severe/profound sudden hearing loss with vestibular symptoms to maximize recovery and are the immediate priority. MRI is important to exclude retrocochlear pathology but can be arranged after treatment initiation. Antiemetics are symptomatic but do not treat the hearing loss. CT is not sensitive for acoustic neuroma.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman who is 16 weeks pregnant presents with sudden hearing loss in her right ear for 1 day, without vertigo or tinnitus. Otoscopy is normal. Audiometry shows a 40 dB sensorineural hearing loss at 4–8 kHz. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discuss with ENT specialist before starting treatment
Reassure that hearing loss often resolves spontaneously
Arrange non-contrast MRI internal auditory meatus
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, corticosteroids should be used with caution due to potential fetal risks, and specialist ENT input is essential to weigh risks and benefits. Starting oral steroids without discussion is inappropriate; intratympanic steroids are not first-line. MRI without contrast is possible but not the immediate first step. Observation is not appropriate given the degree of hearing loss.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of sudden hearing loss in his left ear and severe otalgia. On examination, the left ear canal and conchal bowl show several small vesicles. Neurological examination is normal except for left-sided hearing loss. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
Arrange urgent MRI internal auditory meatus
Refer to dermatology for vesicle management
Perform a Virac test on vesicle fluid
questions.explanation: The presentation is typical of Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus), where early antiviral therapy with aciclovir is the priority to reduce viral replication and improve outcomes. Prednisolone may be added but antivirals are the specific treatment. MRI is not immediately necessary; Virac test is not first-line. Dermatology referral is not needed.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided hearing loss and mild vertigo for 6 hours. She has no other symptoms. Otoscopy is normal. Which examination finding would most urgently warrant an immediate CT head?
questions.explanation: Central nystagmus suggests a brainstem or cerebellar lesion (e.g., stroke or demyelination) causing the hearing loss, which is a life-threatening mimic of idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss and requires urgent imaging. Tinnitus, aural fullness, a positive fistula test, or canal erythema are common in benign causes and do not mandate same-day CT.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with acute bilateral hearing loss, headache, fever, and neck stiffness. What is the most likely cause of her hearing loss?
questions.explanation: Bilateral sudden sensorineural hearing loss with meningeal signs is classic for bacterial meningitis, a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics and steroids. Viral labyrinthitis is usually unilateral and without meningism. Meniere's disease, otosclerosis, and acoustic neuroma do not cause fever or neck stiffness.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with severe right ear pain, purulent discharge, and sudden hearing loss. On otoscopy, granulation tissue is seen in the ear canal. Which complication should be suspected?
questions.explanation: In an immunocompromised or diabetic patient, granulation tissue in the ear canal with severe pain and hearing loss is pathognomonic for necrotising (malignant) otitis externa, a skull base osteomyelitis that can be life-threatening. Acute otitis media, mastoiditis, cholesteatoma, and bullous myringitis do not typically present with canal granulation tissue.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents after a fall onto the left side of his head. He reports sudden hearing loss and notes blood in his ear canal. Which finding on examination would most likely require urgent neurosurgical consultation?
questions.explanation: CSF otorrhoea indicates a breach of the dura (basal skull fracture) with risk of ascending meningitis; this is a neurosurgical emergency. Hemotympanum, tinnitus, vertigo, and facial nerve palsy (if delayed) are common after temporal bone fracture but do not mandate immediate neurosurgical involvement in the absence of CSF leak.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden left-sided hearing loss, severe vertigo, and difficulty walking. On examination, he has left Horner's syndrome, left facial numbness, and ataxia of the left arm. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of ipsilateral Horner's, facial numbness, and limb ataxia with sudden hearing loss and vertigo is classic for lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome, a stroke in the PICA territory). Meniere's disease, vestibular neuritis, and Ramsay Hunt syndrome do not cause Horner's or limb ataxia. Acoustic neuroma typically presents with gradual hearing loss, not acute.
